channel.title: NPR Topics: Health
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: Pakistan ends 'luxury tax' on menstrual products, contraceptives. Will prices drop?
item.description: In Pakistan, taxes on menstrual products can add up. Activists have long worked to change this. Now a new budget wipes out the 18% sales tax. But questions remain about the impact on prices.
item.content:encoded:
In Pakistan, taxes on menstrual products can add up. Activists have long worked to change this. Now a new budget wipes out the 18% sales tax. But questions remain about the impact on prices.

(Image credit: Huizeng Hu/Moment RF)
item.dc:creator: Gabrielle Emanuel

item.title: Winning strategy to prevent diabetes and related chronic diseases
item.description: In the U.S., 115 million adults have prediabetes, which is tied to heart disease and other health problems. A 20-year follow-up to a landmark diabetes study shows lifestyle interventions cut the risk.
item.content:encoded: In the U.S., 115 million adults have prediabetes, which is tied to heart disease and other health problems. A 20-year follow-up to a landmark diabetes study shows lifestyle interventions cut the risk.
item.dc:creator: Allison Aubrey

item.title: Trump further guts Education Dept. by shifting oversight of special ed, civil rights
item.description: The moves to the federal departments of Health and Human Services and Justice, respectively, would further dismantle an agency that President Donald Trump has vowed to close.
item.content:encoded:
The moves to the federal departments of Health and Human Services and Justice, respectively, would further dismantle an agency that President Donald Trump has vowed to close.

(Image credit: Al Drago)
item.dc:creator: Jonaki Mehta

item.title: Live with a partner? You may be sharing more microbes than you think
item.description: A large study finds you may share about a quarter of your oral and gut microbes with the people you live with. Should you worry? We asked the experts.
item.content:encoded:
A large study finds you may share about a quarter of your oral and gut microbes with the people you live with. Should you worry? We asked the experts.

(Image credit: Benambot/iStockphoto)
item.dc:creator: Joseph Kim

item.title: Is a transparent fish the future of brain science? This center is betting on it
item.description: One of the world's leading brain research centers is shifting away from fruit flies and toward a tiny, transparent fish. The goal: to understand how brains control the behavior of an animal or human.
item.content:encoded:
One of the world's leading brain research centers is shifting away from fruit flies and toward a tiny, transparent fish. The goal: to understand how brains control the behavior of an animal or human.

(Image credit: Chie Satou)
item.dc:creator: Jon Hamilton

item.title: Ticks surge in the Northeast
item.description: It's prime tick season, especially in the Northeast and Midwest. The prevalence of ticks is changing the way some people in highly affected areas live their lives.
item.content:encoded: It's prime tick season, especially in the Northeast and Midwest. The prevalence of ticks is changing the way some people in highly affected areas live their lives.
item.dc:creator: Pien Huang

item.title: Most parents track their 18- to 25-year-old kids on their smartphones. Is it healthy?
item.description: A new survey from the University of Michigan asks parents about their use of technology to track their adult children, ages 18-25, including using "always on" location tracking on their smartphones.
item.content:encoded: A new survey from the University of Michigan asks parents about their use of technology to track their adult children, ages 18-25, including using "always on" location tracking on their smartphones.
item.dc:creator: Allison Aubrey

item.title: Inside Ebola country: NPR reports from eastern DR Congo's outbreak zone
item.description: In eastern Democratic Republic of Congo, an Ebola outbreak is reshaping life, with fear spreading faster than information and hospitals turning into places of urgent containment rather than care.
item.content:encoded:
In eastern Democratic Republic of Congo, an Ebola outbreak is reshaping life, with fear spreading faster than information and hospitals turning into places of urgent containment rather than care.

(Image credit: Arsène Mpiana Monkwe for NPR)
item.dc:creator: Emmet Livingstone

item.title: Here's who's missing at the World Cup: Soccer-playing grannies!
item.description: The World Cup isn't the only global soccer event of note. Check out the Grannies International Football Tournament.
item.content:encoded:
The World Cup isn't the only global soccer event of note. Check out the Grannies International Football Tournament.

(Image credit: Samantha Reinders for NPR)
item.dc:creator: Ryan Lenora Brown

item.title: Primary care doctors raise alarm as telehealth companies get involved in obesity drugs
item.description: Telehealth companies offer lifestyle support so people taking obesity drugs can have the most success losing weight. But employers also want the telehealth providers to limit spending on the drugs.
item.content:encoded:
Telehealth companies offer lifestyle support so people taking obesity drugs can have the most success losing weight. But employers also want the telehealth providers to limit spending on the drugs.

(Image credit: Lisa Wiseman for NPR)
item.dc:creator: Sydney Lupkin
